longForm.title: The Hundred Hours: Operation Desert Storm's Ground Campaign, February 24–28, 1991
longForm.excerpt: The Gulf War's ground campaign lasted 100 hours and routed the Iraqi Army. What it did not do — destroy the Republican Guard — shaped the next decade of US policy in the region.
paragraphs: The standard account of the Gulf War ground campaign frames it as a validation of American military doctrine: AirLand Battle, applied at scale, against a conventionally organised opponent who had had six months to dig in. The account is accurate as far as it goes. The 38-day air campaign had degraded Iraqi armour, artillery, and logistics to the point where the ground force encountered, in many sectors, an opponent that had already decided it could not win. The hundred hours required to reach a ceasefire were not the measure of the campaign's efficiency. The question worth asking is what the hundred-hour endpoint left unfinished.

General Norman Schwarzkopf's operational plan — OPLAN 1002-90 in its final form — had been built around a simple deception and a complex envelopment. The deception was designed to convince Iraqi commanders that the coalition main effort would come as an amphibious assault along the Kuwait coast and a direct frontal attack from Saudi Arabia into the Iraqi defensive lines. The actual main effort was a 200-mile displacement to the west, a 'left hook' through the open desert that would position VII Corps and XVIII Airborne Corps to strike the Iraqi operational reserve — the Republican Guard — from the flank and rear. It required moving over 300,000 personnel and their equipment without Iraqi intelligence detecting the shift. They did not detect it.

The air campaign that preceded the ground war ran from 17 January to 23 February 1991. Its effects on Iraqi ground forces were significant but unevenly distributed. Republican Guard divisions in the Kuwaiti theatre — the Hammurabi, Medina Luminas, and Tawakalna armoured divisions — were subjected to sustained interdiction but retained considerable combat capability at G-Day. Forward-deployed Iraqi infantry divisions, by contrast, had been severely attrited and in some cases had lost over 50 percent of their equipment. The strategic assessment, produced after the war by CIA and Defense Intelligence Agency analysts, estimated Iraqi combat effectiveness at G-Day at roughly 50 percent of pre-war levels overall, with wide variation between units. The Republican Guard was at the top of that range.

The ground war began at 4:00 a.m. on 24 February 1991. XVIII Airborne Corps, on the western flank, executed the longest air assault in history: the 101st Airborne Division's helicopters carried soldiers to Forward Operating Base Cobra, 93 miles inside Iraq, establishing a fuel and supply point for the drive to cut Highway 8 — Iraq's main logistics artery to the Kuwaiti theatre. The 24th Infantry Division (Mechanized) and the French 6th Light Armoured Division moved rapidly north. On the coast and in the centre, the Marine Expeditionary Forces and the Arab coalition forces pushed directly into Kuwait, encountering the expected forward defences and, in many cases, mass surrenders.

VII Corps, commanded by Lieutenant General Frederick Franks, was the armoured fist of the operation — three heavy divisions and an armoured cavalry regiment tasked with finding and destroying the Republican Guard. Franks advanced more slowly than Schwarzkopf wanted, a friction point that became a public controversy after the war. Schwarzkopf's position, stated in his memoirs, was that VII Corps should have committed its full weight earlier and faster. Franks's position was that he was managing a force of 146,000 soldiers and 48,000 vehicles across a front of over 100 miles in conditions of limited visibility and communications, and that the risk of a disorganised advance outweighed the time cost of a deliberate one. The documentary record supports neither commander fully. VII Corps was slower than the plan envisioned. It was also operating in conditions the plan had not fully accounted for.

The war's defining armoured engagements occurred on 26–27 February. The Battle of 73 Easting, on 26 February, brought the 2nd Armored Cavalry Regiment into contact with elements of the Republican Guard Tawakalna Division in a featureless desert that offered no cover and no orientation points except the grid reference that gave the battle its name. The regiment's lead troop, Eagle Troop under Captain H.R. McMaster, destroyed approximately 28 T-72 tanks and 16 APCs in 23 minutes. Not one American vehicle was lost. The following day, the 1st Armored Division's Battle of Medina Ridge destroyed approximately 60 T-72 tanks of the Medina Luminas Division in less than an hour. The M1A1 Abrams, firing depleted uranium rounds from a thermal-imaging system that could acquire targets at over 3,000 metres, was not in the same technological category as the T-72 it faced. The battles were not contests; they were demonstrations of a capability gap that the Iraqis had no way to close.

The ceasefire came at 8:00 a.m. on 28 February 1991, exactly 100 hours after the ground war had begun. President George H.W. Bush announced it in an address from the Oval Office. The decision to halt was made by the President and his National Security Advisor Brent Scowcroft, over the objection of at least some theatre commanders who believed another 24 to 48 hours would have allowed the destruction of the Republican Guard formations that were retreating north out of Kuwait. Two of the three Republican Guard armoured divisions — Hammurabi and Medina — escaped substantially intact. They would subsequently be used to suppress the Shia uprising in southern Iraq in March 1991. The uprising had been partly encouraged by American public statements about the desirability of regime change. The survivors of that suppression noted the discrepancy.

Coalition casualties were 292 killed in action, of whom 147 were American. Approximately 35 of the American deaths were attributable to friendly fire — a proportion that reflected both the confusion inherent in a fast-moving armoured battle and the limitations of battlefield identification systems in 1991. Iraqi casualties remain disputed: estimates range from 8,000 to 50,000 killed, with figures of 20,000–35,000 most commonly cited in subsequent analyses. The variation in estimates reflects the difficulty of counting casualties in a fast-retreating force across open desert, rather than any systematic effort at concealment. Approximately 175,000 Iraqi prisoners were taken.

The Gulf War's strategic consequences outlasted its tactical clarity. The survival of the Republican Guard allowed Saddam Hussein to remain in power. The decision to end the war without occupying Baghdad — explicitly justified by Scowcroft and Bush in their 1998 joint memoir as a recognition that occupation would fracture the coalition and produce an unmanageable post-war situation — was revisited and reversed by the Bush administration twelve years later under different assumptions. Whether those different assumptions were better or worse informed than the 1991 judgment is a question that the subsequent record has not made easier to answer. What the 1991 ground campaign established definitively was that the United States Army, properly equipped and trained to the AirLand Battle doctrine developed after Vietnam, could destroy a conventionally organised Soviet-pattern army in open terrain. What it left open was the question of what happened next.

shortForm.title: Nine Percent That Won the War: Precision-Guided Munitions and the Gulf War Revolution
shortForm.excerpt: Only 9 percent of the ordnance dropped in Desert Storm was precision-guided. That 9 percent accounted for roughly 75 percent of strategic target damage. The arithmetic rewrote American air power doctrine.
paragraphs: The Gulf War introduced precision-guided munitions to the nightly news and to defence budget arguments simultaneously. Video footage of GBU-10 Paveway laser-guided bombs entering ventilation shafts and doorways produced an image of surgical air power that post-war analysis partly confirmed and partly complicated. The confirmed part: PGMs achieved a circular error probable — the radius within which 50 percent of munitions land — measured in metres rather than hundreds of metres. Against point targets such as bridges, command bunkers, and hardened aircraft shelters, laser-guided weapons performed as advertised. The complicated part: weather. Laser designation requires a clear line of sight between the designating aircraft and the target. In the overcast conditions that affected roughly a third of Desert Storm's flying days, the precision disappeared.

The Tomahawk Land Attack Missile made its combat debut in Desert Storm. 288 TLAMs were fired in the opening hours of the air campaign, primarily against Baghdad air defence nodes and command infrastructure. The missiles flew pre-programmed routes using terrain-contour matching (TERCOM) navigation and a digital scene-matching terminal guidance system, achieving accuracy well within the weapon's design specifications against fixed targets. The F-117A Nighthawk stealth aircraft, operating without fighter escort and attacking targets in the most heavily defended airspace in the world, flew 1,271 sorties and suffered no losses to Iraqi air defences — a result that validated the low-observable technology investments of the 1970s and 1980s and demonstrated that stealth, combined with precision, could substitute for mass against a sophisticated integrated air defence system.

The post-war assessment, conducted by the Gulf War Air Power Survey under the direction of Eliot Cohen, produced conclusions that the Air Force did not entirely welcome. PGMs constituted approximately 9 percent of total ordnance dropped but accounted for roughly 75 percent of strategic target damage. Conventional iron bombs — the other 91 percent — were effective against area targets such as troop concentrations and supply depots but wasteful against hardened point targets. The survey recommended a systematic shift toward precision weapons that would take 15 years and two more wars to fully implement. The survey's most important finding was methodological: accurate bomb-damage assessment requires sources other than mission reports from the aircraft that dropped the weapons. The Gulf War's BDA was often wrong, and commanders made targeting decisions on the basis of it.

The Gulf War PGM experience established a set of expectations about air power that proved durable and, in certain respects, misleading. The expectation that precision munitions could achieve strategic effects without ground forces was tested in the Balkans in 1999 and found partially wanting — Milosevic eventually conceded, but 78 days of air strikes against a dispersed opponent on complex terrain demonstrated the limitations of precision against targets that could move or hide. The lesson from 1991 was correctly understood as: precision air power is highly effective against fixed, identifiable targets in a permissive or partially permissive environment. It was occasionally misunderstood as: precision air power wins wars by itself. The distinction matters more in some campaigns than others.

sources.title: Gulf War Air Power Survey Summary Report

onThisDay.headline: Battle of the Wilderness Begins as Grant Crosses the Rapidan River
onThisDay.body: On 5 May 1864, Lieutenant General Ulysses S. Grant launched the Overland Campaign by crossing the Rapidan River into the dense woodland known as the Wilderness — the same ground where Stonewall Jackson had flanked Hooker's army a year earlier at Chancellorsville. Lee attacked immediately, hoping to negate Grant's numerical advantage in the tangled terrain. Two days of fighting produced approximately 17,666 Union and 11,125 Confederate casualties with no decisive result. Unlike his predecessors, Grant did not retreat. He ordered his army south toward Spotsylvania.
